reason: Both answers correctly identify the Goodhart's-law dynamic, note that activated users are roughly flat despite the signup surge, and recommend paying on activation plus segmenting referred vs. organic cohorts. They're close in quality. A distinguishes itself with two additional useful moves: (1) telling the reader to check whether organic activation also dropped, guarding against misattributing an unrelated funnel problem to the referral program, and (2) ending with a concrete decision rule (compare cost-per-activated-user against other channels to decide whether to cut or kill the program). A's fraud-detection suggestion (device/payment fingerprints) is also more specific than B's generic 'dupe emails/devices.' B's unique contribution — cash vs. product-credit incentive — is reasonable but secondary and doesn't offset A's sharper diagnostic and decision guidance. Overall A is marginally more useful and complete.
